paper_analysis.objective: To synthesize current evidence on the epidemiology, pathophysiology, causative pathogens, clinical outcomes, and management of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in adults with diabetes and to identify research gaps for future trials.
paper_analysis.method: A narrative review of PubMed, EMBASE, the Cochrane Library, and Web of Science from 1999 to August 2025 was conducted. GRADE was used to rate evidence from 81 selected English-language studies, including randomized controlled trials, cohorts, and meta-analyses.
key_findings: Diabetes increases CAP incidence (relative risk 1.73, 95% CI 1.46-2.04), hospitalization (+30%-50%), and 30-day mortality (odds ratio 1.67, 95% CI 1.45-1.92).
Key drivers include hyperglycemia-induced immune paralysis, pulmonary microangiopathy, ferroptosis, glycation and methylation changes, and gut-lung dysbiosis.
Host-targeted therapy with moderate glycemic control (5-10 mmol/L), continued metformin, and pathogen-directed antibiotics improves survival.
Single-dose PCV20 and annual influenza vaccination prevent approximately 45% of CAP admissions.
limitations: Emerging strategies such as nanozymes, ferroptosis inhibitors, probiotics, and proteolysis-targeting chimeras are still preclinical.
Multicenter randomized controlled trials integrating tight glucose monitoring, novel host-directed agents, and microbiome modulation are needed.
paper_analysis.context: CAP in patients with diabetes is a distinct, more severe entity mediated by metabolic-immune crosstalk.

entities.name: community-acquired pneumonia
entities.description: A severe respiratory infection that occurs in individuals with diabetes, leading to increased hospitalization and mortality.
entities.quote: Patients with diabetes carry a 1.5- to 2-fold higher risk of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) and experience more severe outcomes.

entities.name: diabetes
entities.description: A metabolic disorder characterized by hyperglycemia, which increases the risk and severity of CAP.
entities.quote: Diabetes increases CAP incidence (relative risk 1.73, 95% CI 1.46-2.04).

entities.name: hyperglycemia-induced immune paralysis
entities.description: A condition where high blood sugar levels impair the immune system's ability to fight infections.
entities.quote: Key drivers include hyperglycemia-induced immune paralysis.

entities.name: ferroptosis
entities.description: A form of programmed cell death driven by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation, implicated in the severity of CAP in diabetes.

entities.name: gut-lung dysbiosis
entities.description: An imbalance in the microbiota of the gut and lungs that contributes to the severity of CAP in diabetes.

entities.name: metformin
entities.description: A medication used to manage diabetes that improves survival in CAP patients when continued.
entities.quote: Host-targeted therapy with continued metformin improves survival.

entities.name: PCV20
entities.description: A pneumococcal conjugate vaccine that prevents approximately 45% of CAP admissions.
entities.quote: Single-dose PCV20 prevents approximately 45% of CAP admissions.

properties.name: CAP incidence
properties.quote: Diabetes increases CAP incidence (relative risk 1.73, 95% CI 1.46-2.04).

properties.name: survival improvement
properties.quote: Host-targeted therapy with continued metformin improves survival.

properties.name: CAP admission prevention
properties.quote: Single-dose PCV20 prevents approximately 45% of CAP admissions.

relations.mechanism: Diabetes increases the risk and severity of CAP by causing hyperglycemia-induced immune paralysis, pulmonary microangiopathy, ferroptosis, glycation and methylation changes, and gut-lung dysbiosis.
Continued use of metformin improves survival in CAP patients by managing diabetes and enhancing immune response.
Single-dose PCV20 prevents approximately 45% of CAP admissions by providing immunity against pneumococcal infections.

cross_domain.core_friction: A systemic condition increases susceptibility to a severe infection by disrupting multiple physiological pathways.

mechanism.description: A chronic condition disrupts immune function, cellular integrity, and microbial balance, leading to increased severity of infections.
mechanism.performance: Increases infection incidence by 1.73-fold and mortality by 1.67-fold.

unsolved_tensions.tension: Emerging therapies are still preclinical and require validation.
unsolved_tensions.why_it_matters: Without validated therapies, the severe outcomes of the infection cannot be mitigated.
unsolved_tensions.source_quote: Emerging strategies (nanozymes, ferroptosis inhibitors, probiotics, and proteolysis-targeting chimeras) are still preclinical.

provides.description: Amplifies the severity of infections by disrupting multiple physiological pathways.

requires.description: Restores immune function to mitigate infection severity.
requires.reason: Immune paralysis is a key driver of increased infection severity.

mechanism_components.name: hyperglycemia-induced immune paralysis
mechanism_components.role: Disrupts immune function, increasing infection severity.

mechanism_components.name: ferroptosis
mechanism_components.role: Drives cell death, contributing to tissue damage and infection severity.

mechanism_components.name: gut-lung dysbiosis
mechanism_components.role: Creates microbial imbalance, favoring severe infections.

_meta.title: Community-Acquired Pneumonia in Patients With Diabetes: Narrative Review.
